verbatim_text: So you see this gain in accuracy at any token budget. But more important than that, I think, at least in my head, is this notion where as you get it to sample more tokens, in fact, way more tokens than what it previously sampled during training, then it keeps improving performance. And that's what we call extrapolation of test compute because it's going beyond what it produced during training and it's still able to improve accuracy as it does. In fact, some of the gains behind this test performance is actually rooted in something that we see during training. So here we look at a plot where on the x-axis you have the training compute. This is the compute spent during RL training. This is the amount of flops in the log scale. There is a multilayer of 1E20 here. So it's a ton of flops that you spend during RL training. And all of it is sort of fruitless exploration in online traces where on hard problems, you are measuring this accuracy on hard problems, standard RL barely makes any gain on it. You can throw a ton of compute at it. It barely gets its performance up, even after a lot of iterations. Now, you can use some prior work on auxiliary data that tries to clone this auxiliary data. So you see this initial jump in performance.
